metaDescription:
The practice of living without constantly bracing for impact 

 I spent most of my life with my shoulders up around my ears. Not metaphorically. Actually....
htmlContent:
The practice of living without constantly bracing for impact

I spent most of my life with my shoulders up around my ears. Not metaphorically. Actually. My trapezius muscles were rocks, my jaw was clamped, and my breath lived somewhere in the top inch of my chest. I didn't know I was bracing. I thought that was just how living felt. Tight. Alert. Ready.

Here's the thing about chronic bracing. It works. Until it doesn't. For a long time, that constant state of readiness kept me safe. It helped me predict the unpredictable, scan for danger, and survive environments that weren't safe. My nervous system learned that relaxation meant vulnerability. Letting my guard down meant getting hurt. So I held. And held. And held some more.

But there's a cost to living that way. A cost that shows up in your body, your relationships, your ability to feel joy. You can't selectively numb. When you brace against pain, you also brace against pleasure. Against connection. Against the soft moments that make life worth living. I learned this the hard way, through years of chronic pain, burnout, and a creeping sense that I was watching my own life from behind a pane of glass.

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, would say my nervous system was stuck in a sympathetic state. Fight or flight. Not because there was an active threat, but because my body had learned that threat was the baseline. It didn't know how to come back down. And honestly? Neither did I.

So what does it mean to practice living without bracing? It means slowly, painfully, teaching your body that it can be safe. That the danger has passed. That you don't have to be ready for the blow that never comes. It's not about positive thinking or spiritual bypassing. It's about nervous system work. The kind that happens in the body, not just the mind.

One of the most compassionate reframings of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? by Oprah Winfrey and Dr. Bruce Perry. It asks a different question. Instead of "what's wrong with you," it asks "what happened to you." That shift alone can start to soften the brace.

Why your body doesn't trust safety yet

Here's what nobody told me about recovery. Your nervous system has a memory. It remembers every time you were blindsided. Every betrayal. Every moment when you thought you were safe and then you weren't. Those memories aren't stored in your brain as stories. They're stored in your body as patterns. Your body learned that safety is a trap. A setup for the fall.

So when you try to stop bracing, your body panics. It thinks you're making a catastrophic mistake. It tightens back up. It scans for evidence that you should be on guard. And it finds it, because the world is full of reasons to be scared. Right?

This is where Peter Levine's work on Somatic Experiencing becomes so relevant. Levine showed that trauma isn't the event itself. It's what happens when your nervous system can't complete the survival response. You freeze. You brace. And that unfinished energy gets trapped in your body. The bracing becomes chronic because the body is still trying to finish something that ended years ago.

I remember the first time I tried to just sit still and not brace. It lasted about four seconds. Then my heart started racing, my palms got sweaty, and I felt this overwhelming urge to get up and do something, anything, to regain control. That's not weakness. That's your nervous system doing its job. It's just that its job description was written in a different era of your life.

Think about it. If you grew up with unpredictable caregivers, your nervous system learned that safety was conditional. That you had to earn it by being good, quiet, helpful, invisible. That relaxation was a luxury you couldn't afford. Gabor Mate talks about this extensively. He says that trauma isn't what happens to you, it's what happens inside you as a result of what happens to you. The bracing is the inside part.

So how do you start to unwind it? You don't do it all at once. You don't decide to stop bracing and then just stop. That's like telling someone with a broken leg to just walk it off. The brace is there for a reason. It protected you. Thank it. Then start the slow work of showing your body that the threat has passed.

The micro-moments of un-bracing

I want to tell you about something I learned from Deb Dana, who writes about Polyvagal Theory in practice. She talks about "glimmers." These are micro-moments of safety that your nervous system can learn to recognize. A shaft of sunlight. The sound of your cat purring. The taste of good coffee. These aren't cures. But they're clues. They're evidence that safety exists, even if your body doesn't fully believe it yet.

The practice of living without bracing starts with noticing. Noticing where you hold tension. Noticing when your breath gets shallow. Noticing the stories your mind tells you about why you need to stay ready. And then, maybe, just maybe, you can experiment with letting go for one second. Then two. Then three.

Stay with me here. This is not about achieving a permanent state of relaxation. That's not realistic, and it's not even desirable. Your nervous system needs to be able to mobilize for real threats. The goal is flexibility. The ability to brace when you need to, and to release when you don't. Right now, you might be stuck in the brace position. That's okay. We're working on range of motion.

One thing that helped me was creating actual boundaries. Not the kind you set in your head, but the kind you enforce in your life. A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible is Set Boundaries, Find Peace by Nedra Glover Tawwab. Boundaries reduce the need for bracing because they prevent the violations that trigger the brace response. Less incoming fire means less need for armor.

Another resource that helped me directly address the bracing pattern was The Complex PTSD Workbook by Arielle Schwartz. It's structured and practical. It gives you exercises to do when the bracing gets intense. When you feel that familiar tightening, you can open it and find a grounding technique or a somatic practice that helps you come back to the present.

The micro-moments matter because they build evidence. Each time you notice a glimmer, each time you let your shoulders drop for a breath, each time you choose not to follow the catastrophic thought into the rabbit hole, you're teaching your nervous system a new language. The language of safety.

What happens when you stop bracing

I won't lie to you. When you start to un-brace, things get weird. You might feel more anxious at first. Your body might rebel. You might cry for no apparent reason. You might feel rage that you didn't know was there. That's not regression. That's release. The energy that was held in the brace position is finally moving.

Bessel van der Kolk, author of "The Body Keeps the Score," showed us that trauma lives in the body. So does the bracing. When you start to release it, you're not just relaxing. You're processing. You're completing unfinished survival responses. You're letting your body know that it's safe to feel again.

I remember the first time I cried without bracing. It was terrifying. I was used to crying with a tight chest, holding my breath, trying to make it stop. This time, I let my body do what it wanted. I sobbed. My shoulders shook. I made sounds I didn't recognize. And then it was over. And I felt something I hadn't felt in years. Relief. Actual, physical relief. My body felt lighter. My breathing was deeper. I wasn't waiting for the other shoe to drop.

That moment didn't last forever. The bracing came back. But it came back less intensely. And I knew now that it could leave. That knowledge changed everything.

For many of us who grew up with emotionally immature parents, the bracing started early. A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents by Lindsay Gibson. It helps you understand why you learned to brace in the first place. Why you couldn't relax. Why safety felt conditional. Understanding that context doesn't fix everything, but it helps you stop blaming yourself for being so tightly wound.

The ongoing practice

Living without bracing is not a destination. It's not something you achieve and then you're done. It's a practice. Some days you'll forget completely. You'll catch yourself with your shoulders up, your jaw tight, your breath shallow. And you'll remember. And you'll take one breath where you let go. That's not failure. That's the practice.

I still brace. Especially in new situations. Especially when I'm tired. Especially when something triggers that old familiar feeling of impending doom. But now I notice it faster. I can name it. I can say to myself, "Oh, I'm bracing. That's okay. I don't have to stay here." And sometimes I can't let go. Sometimes the brace is too strong. And that's okay too. The practice isn't about perfection. It's about showing up, again and again, with curiosity instead of judgment.

Janina Fisher talks about the parts of us that hold the bracing patterns. There's a part that believes if you relax, you'll die. Or get hurt. Or lose control. That part isn't wrong. It's just stuck in the past. The work is to thank that part for keeping you alive, and then gently show it that you're in a different place now. That you can handle what comes. That you have resources you didn't have before.

Here's what I want you to take away from this. The bracing is not a character flaw. It's not a sign that you're broken. It's a sign that you survived something. That your body did exactly what it was supposed to do. But now you get to choose. You get to slowly, carefully, compassionately teach your body that the danger has passed. That you can put down the armor. That you can breathe.

It won't happen overnight. It might not happen for years. But every moment you notice the brace and choose to soften, even for a second, is a moment of healing. A moment of return. A moment where you choose life over survival.

And that, right there, is the practice.

Healing Journey Resources

  • What Happened to You? by Oprah Winfrey and Dr. Bruce Perry
  • Set Boundaries, Find Peace by Nedra Glover Tawwab
  • The Complex PTSD Workbook by Arielle Schwartz
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents by Lindsay Gibson
